coredata.dc:title: Utilization of waste biomass of Poa pratensis for green synthesis of n-doped carbon dots and its application in detection of Mn2+ and Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Krishnaiah, Prakash
Atchudan, Raji
Perumal, Suguna
Salama, El-Sayed
Lee, Yong Rok
Jeon, Byong-Hun
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel and sustainable approach was used to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from the waste biomass of Poa Pratensis (Kentucky bluegrass (KB)) by a facile hydrothermal method. The prepared KBNCDs were subjected to various characterization techniques, including X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy to verify the formation of carbon dots and their surface functional groups. The KBNCDs exhibited good hydrophilic fluorescence (FLU) properties with an acceptable quantum yield (7%). The synthesized KBNCDs showed excitation wavelength-dependent FLU emission behavior with strong cyan-blue FLU upon irradiation with 365 nm UV-light. The hydrophilic optical properties of the as-synthesized KBNCDs were used to detect Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions in an aqueous medium with good selectivity and sensitivity. It was found that the FLU of the KBNCDs is quenched in the presence of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions, and the quenching rate was linear with the concentration of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) of KBNCDs with metal ions was calculated using the Stern–Volmer relationship. The LOD values for Fe3+ or Mn2+ ions were calculated as 1.4 and 1.2 μM, respectively with the detection range from 5.0 to 25 μM. Based on these results, this study provides an underpinning for the development of KBNCD as FLU sensors that can be used in aqueous media.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Kentucky bluegrass
Carbonization
Carbon dots
Metal ion detection
Fluorescence sensors

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Advanced nanomaterials and technologies for detection and degradation of environmental pollution hazards, edited by Kyusik Yun, Arthanareeswaran Gangasalam and Saravanan Govindaraju

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of Mn2+ and Fe3+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and ICT, South Korea
Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy
Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning ( KETEP ) grant funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy ( MOTIE ) of the South Korean government (No. 20206410100040 ). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government MSIT ( 2021R1A2B5B02002436 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Methodology adopted for the preparation of KBNCDs from Poa Pratensis (Kentucky bluegrass).
(a) XRD pattern, and (b) Raman spectrum of synthesized KBNCDs.
(a) TEM images of synthesized KBNCDs and (b) the corresponding size distribution graph. (c) and (d) HRTEM images of synthesized KBNCDs with different resolutions.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of synthesized KBNCDs. High-resolution XPS plots of C 1s (b), N 1s (c), and O 1s (d) regions of synthesized KBNCDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the KBNCDs aqueous solutions (inset (a) are digital photographs of KBNCDs aqueous solutions under daylight and UV light). (b) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the KBNCDs aqueous solutions. (c) FLU emission intensity/wavelength of the KBNCDs aqueous solutions by varying the excitation wavelength and (d) the corresponding normalized FLU emission spectra.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous KBNCDs solution at the time of synthesized and after 100 days of storage. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous KBNCDs solution irradiated by a 365 nm UV light at various times.

(a) The FLU intensity of the KBNCDs aqueous solutions in the absence and presence of various metal ions and (b) The FLU intensity ratios ((F0–F/F0)∗100; metal affinity percentage) of the KBNCDs aqueous solutions in the presence of various metal ions (concentration of all metal ions was 1000 μM). (c) Digital photographs of the KBNCDs aqueous solutions in the absence and presence of various metal ions with concentrations of 1000 μM. (d) FLU spectra of KBNCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ ion. (e) FLU spectra of KBNCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Mn2+ ion. (f) The FLU intensity ratios of KBNCDs at 395 nm versus the concentrations of Fe3+ or Mn2+ ions (5–25 μM).
Schematic illustration for the possible quenching mechanism of as-synthesized KBNCDs in the presence of Mn2+ and Fe3+ ions.
Comparison of different fluorescent CDs sensing system with current work.

head.ce:title: Utilization of waste biomass of Poa pratensis for green synthesis of n-doped carbon dots and its application in detection of Mn2+ and Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Earth Resources & Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, 222-Wangsimni-ro, Seongdong-gu, Seoul, 04763, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Earth Resources & Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Occupational and Environmental Health, School of Public Health, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, Gansu Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Occupational and Environmental Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Occupational and Environmental Health, School of Public Health, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, Gansu Province, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors have contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel and sustainable approach was used to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from the waste biomass of Poa Pratensis (Kentucky bluegrass (KB)) by a facile hydrothermal method. The prepared KBNCDs were subjected to various characterization techniques, including X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy to verify the formation of carbon dots and their surface functional groups. The KBNCDs exhibited good hydrophilic fluorescence (FLU) properties with an acceptable quantum yield (7%). The synthesized KBNCDs showed excitation wavelength-dependent FLU emission behavior with strong cyan-blue FLU upon irradiation with 365 nm UV-light. The hydrophilic optical properties of the as-synthesized KBNCDs were used to detect Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions in an aqueous medium with good selectivity and sensitivity. It was found that the FLU of the KBNCDs is quenched in the presence of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions, and the quenching rate was linear with the concentration of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions. The limit of detection (LOD) of KBNCDs with metal ions was calculated using the Stern–Volmer relationship. The LOD values for Fe3+ or Mn2+ ions were calculated as 1.4 and 1.2 μM, respectively with the detection range from 5.0 to 25 μM. Based on these results, this study provides an underpinning for the development of KBNCD as FLU sensors that can be used in aqueous media.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution by heavy metals has become a major concern that has caused many adverse effects worldwide (Luo et al., 2021). One of the major reasons for rapid environmental degradation is that inorganic pollutants are being discarded from the industries to the environment, especially water bodies (Anusuya et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2021). This is mainly due to rapid growth in industrialization, unlawful and inappropriate waste disposal, fertilizers, and pesticides (Liu et al., 2021a,b). Mn2+ and Fe3+ are considered dangerous to both human and animal health among other heavy metals because of their high stability in the ecosystem (Zhao et al., 2020). Mn2+ and Fe3+ ion detection is an essential and imperative task for environmentalists to monitor heavy metal ions in contaminated environmental samples. Therefore, several traditional analytical methods have been used, such as infrared spectroscopy (Chen et al., 2015), inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP OES) (Carvalho et al., 2015), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), and flow injection analysis (Ogunfowokan et al., 2019). However, these techniques have many drawbacks such as expensive instrument costs, complex sample preparation, immobility, and the requirement of skilled labor. Therefore, there is an urgent need for technologies to detect and remove these heavy metal contaminants from the environment. Significant efforts have been made to develop cost-effective and selective fluorescent sensors for identifying heavy metal ions in the environment (Wu et al., 2017). The fluorometric method has many benefits, such as high sensitivity, quick response, and low cost (Zhang et al., 2014).

Fluorescent-based nanomaterials have applications in sensing, imaging, and optoelectronic devices owing to their unique optical properties (Sabet and Mahdavi, 2019). Many studies highlighted semiconductor-based fluorescent quantum dots (QDs) to be one of the best nanomaterials for metal ion sensing applications owing to their exceptional optical sensing, limited emission spectra, tunable size, and color properties (Kaur et al., 2020; Song et al., 2021). These semiconductor-based QDs have major drawbacks of a highly toxic nature even at low quantities, which limits their applications. This limitation of QDs has encouraged many material scientists to develop biocompatible fluorescent QDs. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterial with a size below 10 nm, with significantly high optical properties that can be used in chemo-sensors, biosensors, catalysis, bioimaging, and many other optoelectronic applications (Li et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2015; Tyagi et al., 2016; Mehta et al., 2017).

Biomass is an abundant and renewable material generated from various sources, including forests, agricultural activities, food waste, etc. The global biomass waste generation exceeds 140 Gt, which leads to significant environmental issues (Tripathi et al., 2019). Many researchers have adopted green route synthesis for the generation of nanomaterials (Ahmed et al., 2016; Matussin et al., 2020). (Chen et al., 2021) adopted a one-pot synthesis of nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) for sensitive and selective detection of silver ion and glutathione (GSH). They found that as-prepared N-CDs shows excellent FLU intensity and sensing mechanism was due to oxidation properties of Ag+ leads to formation of silver nanoparticles. Biomass is an important carbon source for many material scientists to produce carbon-based nanomaterials because of its renewability, sustainability, easy availability, non-toxic nature, low cost, and high carbon content (Tade and Patil, 2020). It is already a proven fact that biomass-based CDs possess many advantages such as high bio-compatibility, low cost, selective sensing and photostability over their organic based counterparts (Huang et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2014b).

Various methods have been adopted for the synthesis of CDs, including hydrothermal carbonization, microwave-assisted synthesis, pyrolysis, electrochemical synthesis, combustion/calcination, laser irradiation, chemical oxidation, and ultrasonic synthesis. Hydrothermal carbonization is considered to be a simple and effective synthesis method among others for the generation of highly photoluminescent CDs (Ma et al., 2012, 2019; de Medeiros et al., 2019; Feng and Zhang, 2019; Kalita et al., 2020). In addition, CDs have been proven to significantly improve their fluorescence (FLU) quantum efficiency by functionalization with heteroatoms such as barium, silicon, nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus. Among these different heteroatoms, nitrogen fits the lattice position with carbon-based materials because nitrogen is closest to carbon in the periodic table, and carbon quantum dots (NCDs) with nitrogen doping have been greatly valued over other element-doped CDs (Pu et al., 2020a).

In our study, we describe a simple and green preparation of nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs) using Poa Pratensis (Kentucky bluegrass (KB)) as the carbon source, through a one-step hydrothermal treatment. KB is consists of phenols, flavonoids, tannins, anthocyanins, and so on. Based on this, KB was consumed as a carbon source for synthesizing fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon dots (KBNCDs) with rich nitrogen, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups (Walker and Pesek, 1963). In order to confirm the structural features and surface functionalities of as-synthesized KBNCDs were examined by various analytical techniques. The highly selective and sensitive detection of Mn2+ and Fe3+ ions was performed by FLU quenching technique. This study confirmed that KBNCDs as fluorescent probes are crucial for the detection of Mn2+ and Fe3+ ions. The optical properties of KBNCDs were also thoroughly investigated using spectroscopic techniques.

The waste biomass of Kentucky bluegrass (KB) was collected from the Seoul Olympic Stadium, Seoul, South Korea. Quinine sulfate and ethylenediamine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Republic of Korea). Analytical grade metal salts such as aluminum chloride (AlCl3, Cas#: 7446-70-0), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, Cas#: 7646-85-7), mercury (II) chloride (HgCl2, Cas#: 7487-94-7), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2, Cas#: 7791-20-0), manganese sulfate monohydrate (MnSO4, Cas#: 10034-96-5), chromium (III) chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3, Cas#: 10060-12-5), copper (II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2, Cas#: 10125-13-0), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3, Cas#: 10025-77-1), calcium chloride dihydrate (CaCl2, Cas#: 10035-04-8), lead (II) nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, Cas#: 10099-74-8), cadmium acetate (Cd(CH3OO)2, Cas#: 5743-04-4), and cobalt acetate tetrahydrate (Co(OOCH3)2, Cas#: 6147-53-1) were obtained from Ducksan Chemicals (Republic of Korea). All chemicals were used without further purification, and double distilled (DD) water was used throughout the experiments.

A simple one-step hydrothermal treatment was adopted to prepare KBNCDs according to our previous reports (Atchudan et al., 2018a). First, a well-dried KB biomass was ground well using a mixer grinder and sieved to get a fine powder. Then 0.3 g of KB biomass fine powder was dispersed in 50 mL of double distilled water. To this 0.3 mL of ethylenediamine was added dropwise. The solution was then subjected to ultrasonication for 30 min. The well-dispersed solution was then transferred into a Teflon-coated autoclave. The autoclave was tightly closed and kept in a hot air oven, and carbonization was carried out for 36 h at 180 °C as presented in Fig. 1 . The obtained crude material was filtered through a membrane of 0.22 μm to remove larger particles. Finally, the solution was collected, and solid KBNCDs were obtained by freeze drying and used for further characterizations and analyses.

The morphology and size distribution of the as-prepared KBNCDs were analyzed using a TEM analyzer (Model: JEM-2010 PlusElectron Microscope). TEM samples were prepared on a copper grid with carbon coating by adding two drops of the aqueous KBNCDs solution and then allowed to dry overnight at ambient temperature. XRD studies were carried out, and the patterns were obtained using an X-ray spectrometer (D8 Advance, Bruker) equipped with monochromatized Cu-Kα excitation. The XPS spectra of the as-prepared KBNCDs were recorded using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS K-alpha+, Thermo Fisher Scientific). Raman spectra of the KBNCDs were recorded on an NRS-3100 Raman spectrometer (JASCO, USA) with 532 nm as a laser excitation wavelength. ATR-FTIR analysis was carried out in transmittance mode on a Perkin Elmer Spectrum 100 in the wavenumber range from 400 to 4000 cm−1 by adding 8 scans at a resolution of 8 cm−1 at the core research support center for natural products and medical materials of Yeungnam University. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded from 200 to 800 nm using an OPTIZEN 3220UV spectrophotometer. Emission FLU spectra and excitation were recorded using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The maximum emission intensity was achieved by varying the excitation wavelength. The scan speed and slit width were fixed at 400 nm/min and 5 nm, respectively.

The XRD patterns of the as-synthesized KBNCDs are shown in Fig. 2 (a). The obtained KBNCDs had an amorphous structure, confirmed by a broad single peak at around 22.5°. It was confirmed that this broad reflection is due to graphite (002) interlayer lattice spacing and assigned for the ununiform arrangement of carbon atoms and weak crystalline carbons (Shahshahanipour et al., 2019). Fig. 2 (b) shows the Raman spectra of the as-synthesized KBNCDs, which show two broad peaks of the D-band at 1340 cm−1 and G-band at 1560 cm−1 (Wang et al., 2020b). The D band is linked with defects in the graphite lattice, which are also called dangling bands of carbon atoms (sp3-hybridized carbon networks). The G-band is linked to the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice (Yang et al., 2018). The graphitization degree (ID/IG) can be calculated from the relative intensity ratio of two peaks, i.e., disorder band (D-band) and crystalline graphitic band (G-band), respectively. The ID/IG is approximately 0.96, which indicates a comparatively appropriate degree of graphitization (Wang et al., 2020b). These findings are in agreement with the XRD results (Wang et al., 2014c). Fig. 3 portrays TEM analysis, which was carried out to determine the shape, size, and morphology of the as-synthesized KBNCDs. As seen in Fig. 3 (a), the CDs appeared orbicular in shape and well distributed with a comparatively narrow size distribution. The average diameter of the obtained KBNCDs was approximately 9 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 (b) (Yang et al., 2018). The HRTEM images (Fig. 3 (c) and (d)) indicated that the KBNCDs have a crystalline inner core and amorphous outer shell structure. The measured lattice distance was about 0.21 nm which is close to the typical graphite (100) plane. The prepared KBNCD dots were characterized by FTIR analysis to identify the surface functional groups on the KBNCDs. As shown in Fig. 4 , the absorption peaks appear approximately at 3200–3400 cm−1 because of N–H and O–H stretching vibrations (Sun et al., 2013; Pu et al., 2020b). The absorption bands at approximately 2855 and 2927 cm−1 were attributed to the C–H symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2019; Bhamore et al., 2019). Furthermore, the absorption bands that appeared at approximately 750–900, 1000–1080, 1334, 1420, 1590, and 1650 cm−1 were attributed to the –CH2, C–O–C, C–OH, C–N, CC, and CO functional groups, respectively (Qi et al., 2019). We found that the as-synthesized KBNCDs were highly hydrophilic in nature and well dispersible in water owing to their large number of surface functional groups, as confirmed by FTIR analysis, as discussed above. In addition to the FTIR analysis, XPS measurements were performed to confirm the surface functionalities of the synthesized KBNCDs.

The XPS survey spectrum of the as-obtained KBNCDs (Fig. 5 (a)) shows peaks centered approximately at 286, 399, and 532 eV, which were related to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s elements, respectively. The KBNCDs comprise 63, 31, and 6 atomic percentages of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen at the associated binding energies, respectively. The well-known graphitic C 1s core level peak could be deconvoluted into four different peaks (Fig. 5 (b)) at around 284.69, 285.5, 288, and 290.4 eV, which correspond to C–C, C–OH/C–H, C–N–C/C–O–C, and OC–OH bonds, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2020a, 2020b). Fig. 5 (c) shows the high-resolution N 1s peaks at 397.8, 399.5, and 400.5 eV attributed to C–N–C, C–N–H, and (C3)–N, respectively (Wang et al., 2020a). The oxygen (O 1s) spectrum (Fig. 5 (d)) shows three peaks at 531.5, 533.2, and 535.6 eV, attributed to CO, OC–OH, and H–O–H groups, respectively (Qi et al., 2019; Atchudan et al., 2020c). These XPS findings confirm the presence of oxygen and nitrogen containing functional groups on the surface of the prepared KBNCDs, and the C–N–C bond formation confirms the chemical bonding of the nitrogen atom with the carbon framework. These XPS analysis results were also in agreement with the FTIR results. In Fig. 5 (a) of the survey spectra of XPS analysis, the oxygen (O 1s) peak intensity is higher compared to carbon (C 1s). This may be due to the presence of a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the KBNCDs, which is in agreement with the FTIR and XRD results.

The optical properties of KBNCDs were examined using UV–visible (UV–Vis) absorption and FLU spectroscopic techniques. Fig. 6 (a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the as-synthesized aqueous KBNCD solution, and the absorption spectrum of KBNCDs shows three shoulder peaks at approximately 243, 275, and 325–370 nm. The first two absorption bands (243 and 275 nm) are due to the π→π∗ transition of the conjugated aromatic sp2 domains (CC and CN bonds) from the carbon core (Atchudan et al., 2018a). A broad absorption band between 325 and 370 nm is attributed to the typical n→π∗ transition that occurs due to the non-bonding electron orbitals of the –CO functionality on the surface of KBNCDs. The inset of Fig. 6 (a) depicts the aqueous suspension of KBNCDs as pale yellow under daylight and shows bright cyan (blue) FLU at 365 nm under UV light. Fig. 6 (b) shows the FLU spectra of the maximum excitation (λ ex) and emission (λ em) wavelengths of the synthesized KBNCDs. The FLU spectra showed maximum λ ex and λ em values of 320 and 395 nm, respectively. The FLU emission spectra of KBNCDs at different excitation wavelengths with 10 nm increments were investigated (Fig. 6(c)). In the detailed FLU emission spectra of KBNCDs, the FLU intensity changes with excitation wavelength from 280 to 400 nm. The FLU intensity gradually increased with increasing excitation wavelength. At an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, a maximum emission was observed, and the intensity gradually decreased with a further increase in the excitation wavelength. Furthermore, the detailed FLU emission spectra of KBNCDs at excitation wavelengths ranging from 280 to 400 nm displayed a significant redshift from 370 to 470 nm. The quantum yield of the synthesized KBNCDs was calculated as 7%. Fig. 6 (d), shows the normalized FLU emission spectrum of the KBNCDs. This demonstrates that the FLU emission of KBNCDs is absolutely excitation-dependent, which is one of the intrinsic properties of CDs, unlike typical semiconductor quantum dots. The theoretical concepts behind the properties described above can be the quantum confinement effect, surface trap model, or electronegativity of heteroatoms. Conceivably, the key reason is the presence of various emissive sites with many functional groups such as –CO, C–O–C (epoxy), –N–H, and –O–H on KBNCDs, causing a redshift (Atchudan et al., 2018b; Thongsai et al., 2019).

The stability of synthesized KBNCDs is one of the important properties to assess their potential applications (Wang et al., 2015; Guo et al., 2016; Atchudan et al., 2021). Fig. 7 (a) shows the FLU emission spectra of aqueous KBNCDs solution at different storage times. The FLU intensities of KBNCDs at the time of synthesis and the 100th day of storage are almost similar. These insignificant FLU changes strongly support the prolonging stability of the KBNCDs. Further, the photostability of KBNCDs was examined by continuous 365 nm UV light irradiation. Fig. 7(b) shows the FLU emission spectra of aqueous KBNCDs solution at different irradiation times. The FLU emission intensity showed insignificant decrement even after 60 min of UV light irradiation, confirming the excellent photostability of KBNCDs. These results suggest that the synthesized KBNCDs as a promising candidate for practical applications (Atchudan et al., 2020b).

The synthesized KBNCDs were utilized as a fluorescent sensor to determine some metal ions in aqueous solutions based on their excellent optical properties. Fig. 8 (a) shows the FLU variations of the synthesized aqueous KBNCD solution (blank). After the addition of various metal ions at 1000 μM (such as Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+) at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, changes were observed in the FLU intensities (FLU enhancement or quenching) of KBNCDs. In the presence of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions, the FLU intensities of the KBNCDs were nearly zero (FLU turn-off) at the same concentrations. There are numerous different FLU singularities in the interactions between the KBNCDs and metal ions. In addition, metal ions with KBNCD solutions had different FLU emission intensities after excitation at the same excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Fig. 8 (b) shows the quenching effects (metal affinity) of KBNCDs in the presence of different metal ions. The quenching effects of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions were very strong, as shown in the bar graph in Fig. 8 (b). In addition, Cu2+ showed moderate quenching, while the other metal ions did not show a significant quenching effect. Fig. 8 (c) and Figs. S1(a) and (b) show the corresponding pictorial representations (digital photographs) of the aqueous KBNCD solution in the blank and the presence of different metal ions under illumination with 365 nm UV light. It can be seen that Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions remarkably quenched (turn-off) the FLU of the KBNCDs. Also, Figs. S1(c) and d ) show a pictorial representation of KBNCDs in the absence and presence of different metal ions, respectively, under daylight illumination. The aqueous KBNCD solution with Fe3+ ions showed a slight color change, and in the presence of Mn2+ ions, a significant color change was observed under daylight. Meanwhile, KBNCDs can be used as a colorimetric sensor to detect Mn2+ ions in aqueous media. These results strongly support the selectivity of KBNCDs for Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions. Therefore, the selectivity and sensitivity of the Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions were studied. FLU titration experiments were carried out to investigate the quenching and complexation of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions with the synthesized KBNCDs, as shown in Fig. 8(d) and (e). The FLU intensities of the KBNCDs weakened gradually with increasing metal ion concentrations (Fe3+ or Mn2+) from 0 to 25 μM, but the emission wavelength remained unchanged during this process. Virtuous linear plots (linear correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.997 for Fe3+ and R2 = 0.998 for Mn2+) were observed between the FLU intensity ratio (F0–F/F0) of KBNCDs at an emission wavelength of 395 nm and the impact of metal ion (Fe3+ or Mn2+) concentration (5–25 μM), as shown in Fig. 8 (f). F0 and F correspond to the FLU intensity of the KBNCD solution in the absence and presence of Fe3+ or Mn2+ ions at different concentrations. From the Stern–Volmer relationship, the binding constants of KBNCDs with metal ions were calculated using equation (1) (Atchudan et al., 2020b).
(1)
L O D =
3 σ
S
where σ is the standard deviation (n = 5), and S is the slope of the linear calibration plot. The calculated limit of detection (LOD) is 1.4 and 1.2 μM for Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions, respectively.

The optical performance of waste biomass of Kentucky bluegrass (KB)-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (KBNCDs) to detect Fe3+, and Mn2+ ions were compared with other recently reported works. The data are listed in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanism of Mn2+ and Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: Functionalized fluorescent CD-based nanosensors were designed as FLU turn-off probes that are commonly used to detect various cations and anions in aqueous solutions. The FLU mechanism of CDs remains unclear. However, many studies claim that the FLU quenching of metal ions is due to surface defects, recombination, and the quantum effects of CDs (Zhang et al., 2018). Based on the above analysis and the literature, it is confirmed that the as-prepared KBNCDs contain a negative surface charge and a large number of functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups. It is well known that negatively charged KBNCDs offer an excellent opportunity for binding metal cations, such as Mn2+ and Fe3+, leading to the accumulation of KBNCDs around the Mn2+ and Fe3+ ions by the formation of the complex by coordination (Ye et al., 2017). A schematic representation of the possible quenching mechanism is shown in Fig. 9 . The complex formation between CDs and metal ions causes charge transfer between them due to non-radiative recombination, leading to a decrease in the FLU intensity (Wang et al., 2014a; Zhang et al., 2018; Picard et al., 2019).

After the addition of metal ions in the aqueous KBNCDs solution, the formed complexes (KBNCDs + Fe3+ and or KBNCDs+Mn2+) were examined by FTIR spectroscopy technique. Fig. S2 shows the ATR-FTIR spectra of pure KBNCDs, KBNCDs + Fe3+, and KBNCDs + Mn2+ complexes (ATR-FTIR spectra of pure KBNCDs from Fig. 3(d) has been redrafted in Fig. S2 for comparison). The vibrational absorbance bands (400−4000 cm−1) of KBNCDs + Fe3+ and KBNCDs+Mn2+ complexes display considerable changes than the pure KBNCDs which might be due to new electronic effect (synergism) between KBNCDs and Fe3+ or Mn2+ ions. Specifically, the N–H/C–N, C–H, –COO, and C–OH vibrational band intensities are obviously decreased for the KBNCDs + Fe3+ and KBNCDs+Mn2+ complexes than compared to pure KBNCDs. The broad vibrational bands of KBNCDs + Fe3+ and KBNCDs + Mn2+ complexes are vividly seen in the range of 3250–3450 cm−1 attributed to the presence of O–H which might originate from the physically adsorbed water molecules. Moreover, the O–H vibrational band intensity of KBNCDs + Fe3+ complex is higher than that of KBNCDs+Mn2+ complex this might due to the hydrophilic nature of Fe3+ compared to Mn2+ ions. Overall, the surface-hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine groups of KBNCDs reciprocate with Fe3+ or Mn2+ ions and produce a new electron/energy transfer (ET) which results in the FLU quenching in KBNCDs + Fe3+ and KBNCDs+Mn2+ complexes. This result strongly supporting the proposed mechanism as described in Fig. 9.

We have demonstrated a facile and green synthesis route to prepare fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from waste biomass of Poa Pratensis (Kentucky bluegrass (KB)) by a hydrothermal route. The presence of nitrogen and other functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, –NH2, on the surface of the KBNCDs, were confirmed by FTIR and XPS analyses. The size, morphology, and graphitic nature of the as-prepared KBNCDs were demonstrated by TEM, Raman, and XRD studies. The resulting KBNCDs exhibited bright and excitation-dependent FLU emission behavior with an acceptable QY (7%) in an aqueous medium which remained highly homogeneous for a long period without any precipitation/coagulation. Then for the detection of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions, the synthesized hydrophilic KBNCDs were utilized as fluorescent probes. The studies showed the LOD values of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions as 1.4 and 1.2 μM, respectively without further chemical modification. The formation of coordination bonds between Fe3+/Mn2+ ions and the nitrogen/oxygen-containing moiety on the surface of KBNCDs is proposed for the quenching/sensing mechanism. Moreover, the synthesized KBNCD proved as a “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for the precise detection of Fe3+/Mn2+ ions in water samples. The present study demonstrates that the synthesized hydrophilic KBNCDs can be used as an advanced fluorescent probe for sensing metal ions in an accelerated manner.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Korea Institute of Energy Technology Evaluation and Planning (KETEP) grant funded by the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) of the South Korean government (No.20206410100040). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government MSIT (2021R1A2B5B02002436).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2021.131764.

Prakash Krishnaiah: Conceptualization; Formal analysis, Methodology, and Writing – original draft and Writing – review & editing. Raji Atchudan: Conceptualization; Data curation; Investigation, Formal analysis, and Writing – original draft and Writing – review & editing. Suguna Perumal: Formal analysis and Data curation. El-Sayed Salama: Writing – review & editing. Yong Rok Lee: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Validation and Visualization. Byong-Hun Jeon: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Validation and Visualization, Writing – review & editing.
